data.sentence: Alright, I think we've got everyone now — Robert, Tanya, good to see you both. Thanks for making time today.
Yeah, of course. We've got a lot to get through so let's, uh, let's just jump in.

Absolutely. And I'll just introduce Kevin — he's one of our Solutions Architects and I brought him on today because some of the questions Tanya sent over ahead of this call are a bit more technical and I wanted to make sure we had the right person in the room.
Hey everyone, Kevin O'Brien. Happy to be here and, yeah, looking forward to the conversation.
Hi Kevin. Tanya Desai, Security Engineering. I'll probably be the one throwing the hard questions at you.
Ha, I'd expect nothing less. Bring it on.
So — look, Maria, I'm going to be straightforward with you. March has been... it's been a rough month for us in terms of our confidence in the platform. You know what I'm referring to.
The Detect outage. Yes, absolutely, and I want to address that head on. That's honestly one of the main reasons I pushed for this call rather than just sending an email.

Six hours, Maria. Six hours where we had zero visibility into threat monitoring. For a financial services firm, that's — I mean, do you understand what our risk exposure looks like in a window like that?

I do, and I'm not going to minimize that. It was a serious failure and I think you deserve a clear explanation of what happened and more importantly what's been done since. Kevin, do you want to walk through the technical side?

Sure. So the root cause was a cascading failure in our event ingestion pipeline. Essentially there was a single point of failure in how events were being routed and processed — one node went down and it kind of pulled everything with it. It wasn't graceful at all.
A single point of failure. In a production monitoring system. I have to be honest, that's a pretty basic architectural problem.

You're right, and I'm not going to sit here and defend the original architecture. What I can tell you is that as of about a week and a half ago, we've implemented redundant processing nodes across the ingestion layer and we've added a circuit breaker pattern so that if one component degrades, it fails independently without taking down the whole pipeline.
Can you send us documentation on those changes? Like, actual architecture diagrams, not just a summary email.

Yes, absolutely. I can get you a technical brief with the updated architecture. I'd want to have our engineering team review it first just to make sure everything is accurate and nothing sensitive gets included, but yeah — that's a reasonable ask and we should be able to turn that around within the week.
I'd also want to understand — what's the monitoring on the monitoring, so to speak? Like how do you detect that Detect itself is down?

That's a really good question and it's something we've actually bolstered as part of the remediation. We have independent health checks running on the pipeline components now, separate from the pipeline itself, and those feed into our internal ops alerting. The goal is that we catch a degradation before it becomes a full outage.
Okay. I mean, that sounds reasonable. Robert, I have more questions but I don't want to monopolize—
No, no, keep going. This is exactly what I need to hear.

Alright. So the other thing I wanted to flag — and this is maybe a broader concern — is that during the outage we had no fallback. No secondary alerting, no cached state, nothing. Is there a recommended configuration we should have had in place that we didn't?

Honestly, that's something we want to look at with you. There are configurations within Detect around alert persistence and, um, sort of offline buffering that can help, but those aren't always enabled by default and I think there may be a gap in how we onboarded your team to those features. I'd want to do a config review with you.
Let's put that on the action items list. I'll have Maria set that up separately.
Done. I'll reach out next week to schedule that with Tanya's team.

Okay. So let's talk about the renewal because that's technically why we're here. Our contract comes up end of Q2 and, uh, I'll be transparent — the board is asking questions. They want to know if we have alternatives.
I appreciate you being upfront about that. Can I ask — is the concern primarily around Detect, or is this a broader platform evaluation?

Mostly Detect, yeah. I mean — Protect has been solid, we've had no issues there. Identity is fine, works as expected. Comply is... we're still getting into it honestly, haven't fully utilized that module.

Actually, on that note — Comply v2 is launching April 7th and that's going to include on-demand reporting for SOC 2, PCI DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001. Given you're in financial services that feels like it could be pretty relevant for your audit cycles.
How on-demand are we talking? Like, can I run a PCI DSS report at 9pm on a Friday before an audit Monday morning?

That's exactly the use case. Yes. You'd be able to generate the report, get a formatted output you can hand directly to auditors. We can demo that for you, probably sometime after the 7th once it's live.
That would actually be useful. We have a PCI audit in June so the timing is... yeah, okay, that's worth a look.

Robert, I want to come back to the competitive piece you mentioned. I'm not going to pretend we don't know who you're talking to — SentinelShield's been pretty aggressive in financial services this year. I'd just ask that before any decision gets made, we get a chance to do a full side-by-side with you.

I think that's fair. Look, nobody wants to go through a platform migration — the integration effort alone is a headache. But I need to be able to justify the renewal to my leadership and right now the March outage is a pretty big thing sitting on the ledger.
Understood. What would make the renewal easier to justify? Is it the technical documentation Tanya mentioned, a formal incident post-mortem, SLA terms — what's most helpful?

Honestly? All of the above. But if I'm prioritizing — I want the post-mortem in writing, I want updated SLA language that actually has teeth around uptime for Detect specifically, and I want that config review done before we sign anything.

That's a clear list and I think those are all doable. I'll own getting the post-mortem document drafted — we have the internal one already, it's more about formatting it for external use. The SLA conversation I'll loop in our commercial team on, I want to make sure I'm not committing to terms I don't have authority on.
That's fine. Just don't let it sit for three weeks.
No, I hear you. I'll have a draft post-mortem to you by end of next week and I'll have an initial SLA proposal from our commercial team within two weeks. Does that work?
That works. Tanya, anything else from your side before we wrap?
Just the architecture doc and scheduling the config review. Oh — and the Comply v2 demo. I actually do want to see that.

Great, I'll put all of that into a follow-up summary this afternoon. Robert, Tanya — thank you both for being candid today. I know this isn't the easiest conversation but I'd rather we're talking through it directly than not.
Yeah, same. Appreciate the transparency on your end too. Let's see where we land after the documentation comes through.
Sounds good. Talk soon.
